obligations.text: Providers of high-risk AI systems must establish, implement, document and maintain a risk management system throughout the entire lifecycle of the system, covering identification and analysis of known and foreseeable risks, estimation and evaluation of risks, and adoption of risk management measures.
obligations.notes: Core obligation — should be retrieved by both systems on any 'provider obligations' query.

obligations.text: Providers of high-risk AI systems that train AI models must develop those systems using training, validation and testing data sets that meet quality criteria, including appropriate data governance and management practices covering design choices, data collection processes, data origin, and measures to detect biases.
obligations.notes: Data quality obligation — distinct from risk management.

obligations.text: Providers must draw up technical documentation for a high-risk AI system before it is placed on the market or put into service, and must keep it up to date. The documentation must demonstrate compliance with requirements and contain at a minimum the elements set out in Annex IV.
obligations.notes: Pre-market documentation — foundational.

obligations.text: High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed to ensure sufficient transparency so that deployers can interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. Systems must be accompanied by instructions for use in an appropriate digital format containing concise, complete, correct and clear information.
obligations.notes: Transparency obligation — direction: provider to deployer.

obligations.text: High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed with appropriate human-machine interface tools so that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during use. Human oversight must aim to prevent or minimise risks to health, safety or fundamental rights.
obligations.notes: Human oversight design obligation.

obligations.text: High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed to achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, and must perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle.
obligations.notes: Technical performance obligation.

obligations.text: Providers of high-risk AI systems must: (a) ensure compliance with Section 2 requirements; (b) have a quality management system under Article 17; (c) keep documentation under Article 18; (d) keep logs under Article 19; (e) ensure conformity assessment under Article 43; (f) draw up an EU declaration of conformity under Article 47; (g) affix CE marking under Article 48; (h) register the system in the EU database under Article 49.
obligations.multi_hop_chain: Provider → OBLIGATION → QualityManagementSystem → REQUIRES → Article17; Provider → OBLIGATION → ConformityAssessment → REQUIRES → Article43
obligations.notes: This is the master obligation chain for providers. Multi-hop: Article 16 → Article 17 → Article 43 → CE marking. Neo4j should chain these; Chroma will only surface if they land in the same chunk.

obligations.text: Providers of high-risk AI systems must put a quality management system in place ensuring compliance with this Regulation. The system must be documented in a systematic manner in the form of written policies, procedures and instructions covering: regulatory compliance strategy, design control, data management, post-market monitoring system, serious incident reporting procedures, and an accountability framework.
obligations.notes: Thematic: links to Article 72 post-market monitoring. Tests community-level retrieval.

obligations.text: Any distributor, importer, deployer or other third party is considered a provider of a high-risk AI system and becomes subject to all provider obligations under Article 16 if they: (a) put their name or trademark on a high-risk AI system; (b) make a substantial modification to a placed-on-market system; or (c) modify the intended purpose of a non-high-risk AI system to make it high-risk.
obligations.notes: Classic multi-hop: distributor role-shift to provider through modification. Neo4j advantage: traverse the role-shift relation.

obligations.text: Deployers of high-risk AI systems must take appropriate technical and organisational measures to use such systems in accordance with the instructions for use. Deployers must assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority.
obligations.notes: Deployer-side counterpart to Article 14. Distinguishes deployer from provider obligations.

obligations.text: Prior to deploying a high-risk AI system, deployers that are bodies governed by public law, or private entities providing public services, must perform a fundamental rights impact assessment covering: description of deployment processes, categories of persons affected, specific risks to fundamental rights, human oversight measures, and complaint mechanisms.
obligations.notes: Multi-step: public body deployer → FRIA → human oversight. Tests thematic retrieval of public-sector-specific obligations.

obligations.text: Providers of AI systems that interact with natural persons must ensure that natural persons are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from context. Deployers of emotion recognition or biometric categorisation systems must inform persons exposed to the system.
obligations.notes: General transparency obligation applicable outside high-risk category.

obligations.text: Providers of general-purpose AI models must: (a) draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation including training and testing process and evaluation results; (b) make available information and documentation to downstream AI system providers; (c) put in place a copyright compliance policy; (d) publish a sufficiently detailed summary of content used for training.
obligations.notes: GPAI-specific obligations. Tests cross-actor chain: GPAI provider to downstream AI system provider.

obligations.text: Providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risk must: (a) perform model evaluation including adversarial testing; (b) assess and mitigate systemic risks at Union level; (c) track, document and report serious incidents to the AI Office; (d) ensure cybersecurity protections for the model and its physical infrastructure.
obligations.multi_hop_chain: GPAIProvider(SystemicRisk) → OBLIGATION → AdversarialTesting; GPAIProvider(SystemicRisk) → REPORTS_TO → AIOffice
obligations.notes: Highest-tier GPAI obligation. Multi-hop: systemic risk classification triggers additional obligations chain.

obligations.text: Under the Swiss FADP, personal data must be processed lawfully, in good faith, and proportionately. Data may only be collected for a specific recognisable purpose, may only be further processed in a compatible manner, and must be destroyed or anonymised when no longer required.
obligations.notes: Swiss data protection principles — direct analogue to GDPR Art.5.

obligations.text: Under the FADP, the controller is obliged to arrange data processing in technical and organisational terms so that data protection regulations are respected, taking account of this from the planning stage (privacy by design). The controller must also ensure through default settings that data processing is limited to the minimum required (privacy by default).
obligations.notes: Swiss Art. 7 privacy by design/default — relevant for AI systems processing personal data.

obligations.text: Both the controller and the processor must each maintain a record of their processing activities. The controller's record must contain: identity of controller, purpose of processing, categories of data subjects and personal data, categories of recipients, retention period, data security measures, and cross-border disclosure details.
obligations.notes: FADP Art. 12 — processing record obligation.

obligations.text: Private controllers with registered office or domicile abroad must appoint a representative in Switzerland if they process personal data of persons in Switzerland on a large scale, regularly, and in a manner posing a high risk to data subjects, in connection with the offer of goods or services or monitoring of behaviour.
obligations.notes: Relevant for EU companies processing Swiss resident data in AI systems.

obligations.text: Under the Swiss FADP, the controller must inform the data subject about any automated individual decision that has a legal consequence or a considerable adverse effect. The data subject may request that the automated decision be reviewed by a natural person.
obligations.notes: Swiss equivalent of GDPR Art. 22. Critical for AI systems making credit, employment or benefits decisions.

obligations.text: If data processing using new technologies is likely to result in a high risk to the data subject's personality or fundamental rights, the controller must carry out a data protection impact assessment beforehand, covering a description of the planned processing, evaluation of risks, and protective measures.
obligations.multi_hop_chain: Controller → HIGH_RISK_PROCESSING → TRIGGERS → DataProtectionImpactAssessment → REQUIRES → RiskDescription
obligations.notes: Swiss DPIA obligation. AI systems using new technologies that pose high risk trigger this.

obligations.text: The controller must notify the FDPIC of any data security breach likely to lead to a high risk to the data subject's personality or fundamental rights as quickly as possible, specifying the nature of the breach, its consequences, and the measures taken or planned. The processor must notify the controller of any breach as quickly as possible.
obligations.notes: Swiss breach notification chain: Processor notifies Controller who notifies FDPIC.

obligations.text: Personal data may only be disclosed abroad if the Federal Council has decided the foreign legislation guarantees adequate protection, or if adequate protection is guaranteed by: an international treaty, data protection clauses in a contract notified to the FDPIC, standard data protection clauses approved by the FDPIC, or binding corporate rules approved by the FDPIC.
obligations.notes: Swiss adequacy framework — comparable to GDPR Ch. V but with Swiss-specific FDPIC role.

obligations.text: The Swiss Federal Administration must ensure that responsibility for AI-supported decisions rests with humans. AI systems must not be used to make final autonomous decisions in matters with significant impact on individuals without human accountability.
obligations.notes: Core principle from Swiss AI Guidelines — human-in-the-loop for public decisions.

obligations.text: The Swiss Federal Administration must ensure transparency in its use of AI. Citizens and affected persons must be informed when AI systems are used in decisions affecting them, enabling them to understand and contest such decisions.
obligations.notes: Public-sector transparency obligation — complements EU AI Act Art. 50 in Swiss context.

obligations.text: The Swiss Federal Administration must ensure that AI systems used are reliable, safe and of high quality. This requires systematic testing, evaluation, and monitoring throughout the system's lifecycle, and clear procedures for dealing with failures.
obligations.notes: Swiss quality/safety obligation for federal use of AI — lifecycle perspective.

obligations.text: The Swiss Federal Administration must ensure compliance with data protection law, in particular the FADP, when processing personal data with AI systems. The principle of data minimisation must be respected, and AI systems may only process the personal data strictly necessary for their intended purpose.
obligations.notes: Cross-document bridge: Swiss AI Guidelines to FADP compliance. Tests cross-document linking.

obligations.text: The Swiss Federal Administration must ensure that AI systems do not produce discriminatory outcomes. Before deployment, systems must be examined for potential bias, and ongoing monitoring must check that no systematic disadvantaging of population groups occurs.
obligations.notes: Swiss anti-discrimination obligation for AI — connects to EU AI Act data governance (Art.10).

obligations.text: Switzerland must consider alignment with the EU AI Act as part of any future AI regulatory framework, particularly in relation to conformity assessment procedures and market access through the Switzerland-EU Mutual Recognition Agreement (MRA). Swiss companies exporting AI-embedded products to the EU must comply with EU AI Act obligations regardless of the Swiss domestic framework.
obligations.multi_hop_chain: SwissCompany → EXPORTS_TO → EUMarket → SUBJECT_TO → EUAIAct(ConformityAssessment) → REGARDLESS_OF → SwissLaw
obligations.notes: Cross-document, cross-jurisdiction bridge. Tests the hardest multi-hop cross-document reasoning.
